prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old woman.
 
 I saw blood when I wiped and I'm scared it could be colon cancer--my uncle died of that.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: Over the last month, I've noticed bright red blood on the toilet paper a few times after bowel movements. It's not mixed into the stool. I've also been constipated and straining sometimes. No abdominal pain, no black stools, and no weight loss.
 - History: No family history of colon cancer that I know of.
 - Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 66
 - Exam: No exam done yet.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Hemoglobin: 14.4 g/dL (13.5-17.5) normal
 - MCV: 90 fL (80-100) normal
 - Ferritin: 60 ng/mL (30-400) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
  I have young kids at home and can't leave them to go to the hospital.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Bright red blood on the toilet paper — not mixed into the stool — in a young person who has been straining with constipation is most commonly caused by hemorrhoids or a small anal fissure (a tiny tear in the skin around the anus). Both are very common and are made worse by straining. Your labs are reassuring: your hemoglobin is solidly normal, meaning you are not losing significant blood; your MCV and ferritin are also normal, so there is no sign of chronic blood loss or iron deficiency. Your vitals are normal too. You mentioned your uncle died of colon cancer, but you also said you have no known family history of colon cancer — at 27 with these findings, colon cancer is extremely unlikely. That said, any rectal bleeding deserves a proper evaluation. A routine visit with your doctor in the next few weeks for an examination (likely a simple rectal exam) would be appropriate to confirm the cause and discuss treatment.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Bright red blood on the toilet paper (not mixed into the stool) in a 27-year-old woman with constipation and straining is most commonly caused by hemorrhoids or a small anal fissure (a tiny tear near the anus). These are very common and usually not dangerous. Your labs are reassuring: your hemoglobin is normal, meaning you're not losing significant blood; your MCV is normal, ruling out the type of anemia seen with chronic blood loss; and your ferritin is healthy. You have no red-flag symptoms like black/tarry stools, unexplained weight loss, abdominal pain, or a family history of colon cancer (your uncle's case is noted, but you listed no known family history). Colon cancer at age 27 without risk factors is extremely rare. That said, any rectal bleeding deserves a proper examination by your doctor to confirm the cause and rule out anything else. Increasing fiber and water intake may help in the meantime.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%
